differential.evidence_basis: Coryzal onset 5 days ago (<10 days), bilateral facial pressure worse on bending, purulent discharge, hyposmia and post-nasal drip — the classic viral rhinosinusitis picture.

Purulent discharge and facial pain raise it, but duration <10 days, steady (not biphasic) course and only low-grade fever make it unlikely as the current diagnosis; it is the main condition to reassess at day 10 or on double sickening.
Upper-tooth ache could be dental, but the pain is diffuse across the sinus distribution rather than localised to a single decayed tooth, with no focal gingival swelling.
History alone (coryzal prodrome, <10 days, positional facial pressure, hyposmia, post-nasal drip) is sufficient for a working clinical diagnosis.
Diffuse maxillary pressure matching the sinus rather than a single tooth, and no localised gingival swelling or clearly decayed tooth.
Deep throbbing infective pain with purulent rhinorrhoea and low-grade fever, not the sharp electric-shock paroxysms of trigeminal neuralgia.
Orbital and intracranial red-flag screen negative on history/video; the working diagnosis stands and is telehealth-manageable with safety-netting.

The feared complication of sinusitis: excluded here by absence of periorbital swelling/redness, diplopia, pain on eye movement or vision change — but definitive exclusion needs in-person examination, so it drives the mandatory safety-net.
Rare intracranial spread: screened negative (no severe unremitting headache, neck stiffness, photophobia or confusion), retained as a hard safety-net trigger.

Self-limiting viral rhinosinusitis at its symptomatic peak; management is symptomatic pain control and drainage with antibiotic avoidance, plus safety-netting for orbital/intracranial complications and for bacterial evolution beyond day 10.

red_flags.description: Orbital involvement — periorbital swelling/redness, proptosis, painful or restricted eye movements, diplopia or reduced vision (orbital/periorbital cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis is a sight- and life-threatening complication of sinusitis needing emergency IV antibiotics and imaging; it cannot be definitively excluded by telehealth, so its features must be safety-netted as an immediate 000/ED trigger.

red_flags.description: Intracranial extension — severe unremitting headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered consciousness or confusion (meningitis, intracranial abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rare but catastrophic spread of sinus infection; any of these features mandates immediate emergency assessment.

red_flags.description: Biphasic 'double sickening' — initial improvement then sharp worsening with high fever — or symptoms persisting/worsening beyond 10 days (evolution to acute bacterial rhinosinusitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks the point at which antibiotic therapy and in-person review become appropriate; currently absent, supporting the viral diagnosis and antibiotic avoidance.

pathophysiology_summary: A viral upper respiratory infection inflames the nasal and paranasal sinus mucosa, causing mucosal oedema that obstructs the osteomeatal complex. Impaired drainage and ciliary clearance trap secretions, producing the pressure sensation, positional pain (worse on bending as hydrostatic pressure rises), purulent-appearing discharge (neutrophils and sloughed epithelium, not necessarily bacterial), hyposmia and referred maxillary dental ache via shared trigeminal innervation. Most cases are viral and self-limiting, peaking in the first week and resolving over 10-14 days as mucosal oedema settles and drainage is restored; a minority develop secondary bacterial infection, and rarely infection spreads to the orbit or intracranial compartment. Discoloured discharge and facial pain within the first 10 days do NOT reliably indicate bacterial infection and do not by themselves justify antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming thick yellow-green discharge equals bacterial infection and prescribing antibiotics; discoloured mucus is common in uncomplicated viral rhinosinusitis and colour alone does not predict bacterial cause within the first 10 days.

Treating severe, distressing, analgesia-refractory facial pain as an emergency and sending the patient to the ED; the pain is severe but the condition is benign and telehealth-manageable once orbital/intracranial red flags are screened negative.

Reassuring the patient without explicitly safety-netting for eye swelling, double vision, vision loss, severe headache, neck stiffness or confusion — the rare complications that turn a benign sinus infection into an emergency.
Failing to cap the topical decongestant at about 3 days, risking rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Sinus tenderness on palpation and percussion over the maxillary and frontal sinuses
Anterior rhinoscopy findings (erythematous oedematous mucosa, purulent discharge in the middle meatus)
Objective extraocular-movement testing and formal periorbital examination to exclude orbital cellulitis
Exact vital signs beyond a patient-reported home thermometer reading (measured BP, HR, RR, SpO2)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reach a working clinical diagnosis from history and video, provide symptomatic self-care (optimised simple analgesia, short-course topical decongestant, saline nasal irrigation), explicitly avoid antibiotics at this stage, and deliver conservative safety-netting: immediate 000/ED for orbital or intracranial features, and prompt in-person GP review for double sickening, high fever, or failure to improve by day 10.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the working diagnosis (classic viral picture). The real risks are OVER-action (unnecessary antibiotics or ED referral) and UNDER-safety-netting (failing to flag the orbital/intracranial complications that cannot be excluded remotely).
